title: ETH-strike premium payouts revert because WETH unwrapping is blocked by `receive()`
claim: The contract only accepts plain ETH when `msg.sender == _exchange`, but `_sellACOTokens` unwraps WETH by calling `IWETH(weth).withdraw(...)`. WETH sends ETH from the WETH contract itself, so `receive()` reverts during the unwrap path whenever the strike asset is ETH and the sale proceeds arrive as WETH.
impact: Writes that rely on WETH proceeds for ETH-settled markets become unexecutable, causing a persistent denial of service for those option sales.
paths: User calls `write(...)` for an ACO whose `strikeAsset()` is `address(0)`.
The exchange sale leaves WETH in `ACOWriter`.
`_sellACOTokens` calls `IWETH(weth).withdraw(wethBalance)`.
WETH sends ETH to `receive()`, but `msg.sender` is the WETH contract rather than `_exchange`, so the transaction reverts.

title: Unvalidated `acoToken` metadata can be abused to sweep arbitrary ERC20 balances held by the writer
claim: `write()` trusts any caller-supplied `acoToken` for both `collateral()` and `strikeAsset()` and never verifies that it is a genuine ACO token. A malicious contract can report any ERC20 as the strike asset, then make `_sellACOTokens` transfer this contract's entire balance of that token to the caller.
impact: Any ERC20 balance that ends up in `ACOWriter`—including accidentally transferred tokens, residual premiums, or stranded assets from broken flows—can be stolen permissionlessly by calling `write()` with a crafted fake token.
paths: Attacker deploys a fake contract implementing the `IACOToken` surface.
The fake token returns attacker-chosen values for `collateral()` and `strikeAsset()`.
`write()` accepts the fake token and proceeds into `_sellACOTokens`.
`_sellACOTokens` reads the fake `strikeAsset()` and calls `_transferERC20(token, msg.sender, balanceOf(token, this))`, sending the writer's full balance of that ERC20 to the attacker.

title: Premium and ETH payouts use global contract balances, letting the next caller drain leftovers
claim: After the exchange call, `_sellACOTokens` pays `msg.sender` the entire balance of the reported strike asset and then the entire ETH balance of the contract, without separating proceeds generated by the current write from assets that were already present.
impact: Any residual strike tokens, exchange refunds, or ETH forcibly sent to the contract can be stolen by whichever user calls `write()` next, redirecting funds away from their rightful owner.
paths: Residual ETH or strike-asset tokens remain in `ACOWriter` from a prior operation, accidental transfer, or forced ETH send.
A later user calls `write(...)`.
`_sellACOTokens` transfers `balanceOf(token, this)` or `address(this).balance` to the new caller.
The leftover funds are drained by the new caller instead of the party who originally owned them.

title: Newly minted options are sent to the caller, but the sale logic only sells the writer's old balance
claim: Both mint paths create ACO tokens for `msg.sender`, yet `_sellACOTokens` measures and approves only `balanceOf(address(this))`. As a result, the freshly written options are never the tokens being sold; only any pre-existing ACO tokens already stuck in the writer are exposed to sale.
impact: The advertised write-and-sell flow is broken. Honest users can deposit collateral and receive options without any automatic sale, while unrelated ACO balances already held by the contract can be sold by a later caller who keeps the premium.
paths: User calls `write(validAcoToken, collateralAmount, exchangeAddress, exchangeData)`.
`mintToPayable` or `mintTo` mints ACO tokens to `msg.sender`, not to `ACOWriter`.
`_sellACOTokens` computes `acoBalance = balanceOf(acoToken, address(this))`, which excludes the newly minted user balance.
If the writer already held that ACO token, those unrelated tokens are sold and the caller receives the proceeds.

title: Using `transfer` for final ETH payout lets contract callers DOS writes that return ETH
claim: The final ETH payout uses `msg.sender.transfer(address(this).balance)`, which forwards only 2300 gas. If the caller is a smart contract wallet or router whose receive/fallback needs more gas or deliberately reverts, the entire write reverts whenever ETH must be returned.
impact: Contract-based users and integrators can be permissionlessly denied service on any path that leaves ETH in the writer, reducing composability and making otherwise valid writes fail.
paths: A smart-contract caller invokes `write(...)`.
The operation leaves ETH in `ACOWriter` after minting and exchange settlement.
`msg.sender.transfer(...)` executes with a 2300-gas stipend.
The caller cannot accept ETH under that stipend, so the whole transaction reverts.
